summary: A function f(x) is a rule: plug in a value for x, follow the rule, get one output — evaluating and composing functions is just careful substitution.

formulas.name: Evaluation
formulas.expression: f(a) = \text{substitute } x=a \text{ into the rule for } f(x)

formulas.name: Composition
formulas.expression: f(g(x)) = \text{evaluate } g \text{ first, then plug that result into } f

explanation: To evaluate \(f(a)\) for a function like \(f(x)=x^2+3x\), substitute \(a\) everywhere \(x\) appears: \(f(2)=2^2+3(2)=10\). Composition \(f(g(x))\) means evaluate the inner function first, then feed that result into the outer function — work from the inside out. The GRE sometimes defines a custom operation with unusual notation (like \(a\star b = a^2-b\)); treat it exactly like a function definition and substitute carefully, paying close attention to which variable goes where. A common error in composition problems is evaluating the functions in the wrong order or substituting into the wrong function first.
